coredata.dc:title: Physico-chemical properties of Ali-HexA DES -kerosene mixture and its use in solvent extraction and separation of Li/Co/Ni
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, Pratyasha
Mishra, Sujata
coredata.dc:description: Ali-HexA Deep eutectic solvent (DES) was synthesized using hexanoic acid as a hydrogen bond donor and Aliquat 336 as a hydrogen bond acceptor was confirmed by FTIR and proton NMR spectral studies. Density, molar volume, dielectric constant, refractive index, and ultrasonic velocity of binary mixtures of DES and kerosene have been determined at various mole fractions of DES. The excess properties were calculated using different model equations. The solvent extraction of Li, Co, and Ni from simulated lithium-ion battery waste solution using DES in kerosene under varying experimental conditions has been undertaken to know the suitableness of the novel DES extractant-diluent pair.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ali-HexA DES
Kerosene
Physico-chemical properties
Mixing models
Solvent extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of aqueous and organic phases
Extraction of Li, Co, and Ni
Variation of shaking time and DES mole fraction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation illustrating the structural composition of Ali-HexA DES.
FTIR spectra of Aliquat 336:Hexanoic acid(1:2 molar ratio) DES and DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess volume versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess dielectric constant versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess refractive index versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess ultrasonic velocity versus mole fractions of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess inter molecular free length versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess isentropic compressibility versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of excess acoustic impedance versus mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
Plot of Kirkwood correlation factor (g) against mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in kerosene.
3D surface plot of percentage of extraction versus shaking time variation and mole fraction of DES and at (a) pH 2 (b) pH 5.5 for Ni(II).
3D surface plot of percentage of extraction versus shaking time variation and mole fraction of DES at (a) pH 2 (b) pH 5.5 for Co(II).
3D surface plot of percentage of extraction versus shaking time variation and mole fraction of DES at (a) pH 2 (b) pH 5.5 for Li(I).
Schematic Representation of separation of Ni/Co/Li using Ali:HexA DES diluted in kerosene.
A, B, C and D correspond to Ali:HexA(1:2) DES, Ali:HexA DES diluted with kerosene, Ni(II), Co(II) and Li(I) loaded aqueous solution, aqueous and organic phase after extraction, respectively.
Application of DES for extracting different metals from battery waste.

row.entry: Neither of the two DESs demonstrated high selectivity for Li extraction, as they extracted less than 30 % of the Li. However, when it came to the extraction of Ni, DecA:Lid (2:1) showed superior selectivity. The extraction efficiency for Ni improved as the pH and O/A ratio increased.

Three distinct battery types were employed to segregate Ni, Co, Li, and Fe. Following leaching with HCl at a temperature of 80 °C, using a 2 M HCl solution for 6 h with a solid-to-liquid ratio of 1:25, all the metals were successfully separated using a crossed current extraction method. The extraction efficiency for all the valuable elements exceeded 98 %.

To achieve full dissolution of Co(II) and Mn(II), leaching was conducted at a temperature of 180 °C for 20 h. The extraction efficiency of the ChCl:EG(1:2) DES was 90 % for Co(II) and 10 % for Ni(II). Adding 0.5 M C2H2O4, cobalt was precipitated as oxalate and subsequently used to produce Li2CO3 cathode material.

At a temperature of 140 °C for 10 min, the leaching efficiency without any reductant was determined as 99.5 % for Li, 99.6 % for Co, 99.7 % for Ni, and 99.1 % for Mn. In the case of the BCl:EG DES, the leaching rates under the same conditions were significantly lower: 7.40 % for Li, 0.02 % for Co, 0.17 % for Ni, and 0.14 % for Mn.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Description of chemicals employed in this investigation.
Physical properties of Aliquat 336, n- hexanoic acid, kerosene, and Ali:HexA(1:2) DES at 298.15 K.
Equations for excess properties, dielectric constant, refractive index and ultrasonic velocity and different mixing models.

head.ce:title: Physico-chemical properties of Ali-HexA DES -kerosene mixture and its use in solvent extraction and separation of Li/Co/Ni

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Technical Education and Research (FET), Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan Deemed to be University, Khandagiri Square, Bhubaneswar-751030, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Technical Education and Research (FET)
Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Technical Education and Research (FET), Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan Deemed to be University, Khandagiri Square, Bhubaneswar-751030, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ali-HexA Deep eutectic solvent (DES) was synthesized using hexanoic acid as a hydrogen bond donor and Aliquat 336 as a hydrogen bond acceptor was confirmed by FTIR and proton NMR spectral studies. Density, molar volume, dielectric constant, refractive index, and ultrasonic velocity of binary mixtures of DES and kerosene have been determined at various mole fractions of DES. The excess properties were calculated using different model equations. The solvent extraction of Li, Co, and Ni from simulated lithium-ion battery waste solution using DES in kerosene under varying experimental conditions has been undertaken to know the suitableness of the novel DES extractant-diluent pair.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Deep eutectic solvents were developed as more environmentally friendly option compared to volatile organic solvents as well as ionic liquids in order to achieve low carbon emission and sustainability. These solvents primarily consist of a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) and a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA). When these constituents are mixed, they form intermolecular hydrogen bonding between themselves. Eutectic mixtures display a melting point less than those of their respective constituents and possess distinctive solvent properties [1]. Hydrogen bond acceptors in DESs typically include quaternary ammonium salts, amines, and amino acids. Hydrogen bond donors are commonly alcohols, organic acids, and amides. DESs show resemblances to ionic liquids in terms of low volatility, high thermal stability, and effective solvation ability. However, they are more cost-effectively manufactured compared to the ionic liquids [2,3]. Molecular interaction studies are accountable for the specific properties in the case of liquid mixtures. Several electric and refractive characteristics of molecules can be ascribed to the competitive influences of nuclei with various charges exist or to the competition control exerted by a nucleus and the effect of the externally applied field. The former may give rise to an electric dipole moment while the latter may result in attributes like refractive index and optical activity [4]. The physico-chemical properties of different types of DES have been reported by several researchers. In 2015, Van Osch et al. [5] introduced the concept of hydrophobic DES which possess unique characteristic of low miscibility with water. They also examined the physicochemical properties of six hydrophobic DESs, composed of a quaternary ammonium salt as HBA and decanoic acid as HBD at room temperature. Among these six DESs, DecA:N8888-Br (2:1) exhibited the highest density of 942.2 kg/m3, while DecA:N8881
Cl (2:1) had the highest viscosity of 783.41 mPa.s. Notably, the density values of these six hydrophobic DESs were less than those of most hydrophilic DESs. In their research, Prusty et al. [6] examined the density, refractive index, and dielectric constant of an Aliquat336:Glycerol DES diluted with kerosene. The study was conducted within a temperature range of 298–328 K. According to their findings, as the temperature increased, both the density and dielectric constant values of the DES-kerosene decreased. Additionally, the refractive index value exhibited an increase with rising in temperature. Milevsky et al. [7] investigated the relationship between density, viscosity, and refractive index of a hydrophobic DES composed of thymol as the HBD and Aliquat 336 as the HBA in a molar ratio of 0.55:0.45. The study encompassed a temperature range of 288 K to 333 K. According to their reports, as the temperature increased, the density, viscosity, and refractive index of the DES exhibited a gradual decrease. Specifically, at room temperature, the density of the DES was measured as 0.90 g/cm3, while the viscosity was determined to be 1502.04 cP. Adeyemi et al. [8] conducted a study focusing on the physicochemical properties of hydrophobic DESs within a temperature range of 293–358 K. These include various compositions such as Menthol:Thymol (1:1–4), Menthol:Hexanoic acid (1:2), Menthol:Octanoic acid (1:2), and Menthol:Decanoic acid (1:2). They observed that as the temperature increased, the density decreased for all the DESs. It was also noted that an inverse relationship exists between the carbon chain length of the alkanoic acid and the density of DESs. Among the seven DESs examined, Menthol-Thymol (1:2) exhibited the highest viscosity value of greater than 30 mPas at room temperature.

Li-ion batteries are in high demand across various industries, including power tools, medical devices, aerospace, aviation, energy storage systems, portable electronics, electric vehicles, and backup power. Their versatility, high energy density, and rechargeable nature make them the preferred choice for efficient and reliable energy storage solutions. Recovery of metal values from battery waste using green solvents like DESs is one of the prime areas of research in recent days due to the piling up of large amounts of E-waste. Table 1 enlisted the application of DES to extract different metals from the battery wastes.

Milevskii et al. have used Aliquat 336:L-menthol of 3:7 molar ratio for the separation of Li(I), Co(II), Ni(II), Mn(II), and Fe(II) which is of quite higher concentration. They added relatively higher concentrations of HCl (3 mol/L) and 5 mol/L LiCl into the aqueous solution for the extraction purpose [13]. In another study conducted for the separation of Co and Ni, Milevsky et al., used Aliquat 336:timol (1:5) along with higher concentrations of HCl and LiCl (7 mol/L) to recover only 32.31 % of Ni(II) [7]. Mokhodoeva et al. investigated the isolation and extraction of platinum group elements through the utilization of tetraoctylammonium bromide and hexanoic acid as DES. The study revealed very high extraction efficiency for Pt and Pd in the presence of 0.1 M HCl [14]. As reported in literature Aliquat 336 has been used as a highly effective HBA in the DES. As evident from the previous studies elaborated above, the extraction was either carried out from highly acidic medium or in many cases high operating temperature was used. DESs used were mostly in pure form leading to the consumption of high amounts of chemicals.

In this study, the combination of Aliquat 336: HexA as a DES was explored. This combination was chosen because DESs consisting of quaternary ammonium salt and carboxylic acid have demonstrated good efficiency in extracting both alkali and transition metals and there has been relatively little exploration involving this particular DES. This work aims to examine the physico-chemical properties of Ali:HexA(1:2) DES diluted in kerosene at room temperature which is very vital to assess its extraction efficiency. The present study also aims at the use of room temperature, low acid conditions and diluted form of Ali-HexA DES to examine the extraction performance of the DES for Li, Co, and Ni from simulated Li-ion battery waste under different conditions which includes shaking time, pH, and DES molarity.

All the substances used in this investigation were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. KCl was utilized as a salt additive in the aqueous phase, while readily accessible kerosene was employed as a diluting agent for DES in the organic phase. The chemicals used in the investigation are described in Table 2 .

Aliq:HexA DES was synthesized by combining 1:2 molar ratio of Aliquat 336 and hexanoic acid at 60 °C. The mixture was continuously stirred until a uniform mixture was formed [15]. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and stored overnight in a airtight container to avoid crystallization and absorption of moisture. Fig. 1 shows the schematic depiction of Ali:HexA(1:2) DES. To record the FTIR and NMR spectra of DES,Perkin Elmer UTAR spectrometer and JNM-ECZ400S/L1 were used respectively. The FTIR spectra of Ali:HexA(1:2) DES (Fig. 2 ) reveal several shifting in peak positions of the stretching frequencies of functional groups. In hexanoic acid, the stretching frequency of OH was noticed at 3436 cm−1, while the peak at 2924 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching of CH bonds. The CO stretching frequency of n-hexanoic acid undergoes a shifting from 1293 cm−1 to 1172 cm−1 [16]. The NCH3 stretching frequency of Aliquat 336 shifts to 1465 cm−1 when it interacts with the OH of hexanoic acid. These observations strongly indicate the formation of Ali:HexA DES. The details of NMR spectra has been given as Fig. S1 in supplementary material.

The physical properties like density, refractive index, dielectric constant, and ultrasonic velocity of Aliquat 336, n-hexanoic acid, and Ali:HexA(1:2) DES were measured at 298.15 K and have been provided in Table 3 .

Equations used to evaluate the properties and various mixing models to calculate volume dielectric permittivity, refractive index, and ultrasonic velocity for Ali:HexA DES and kerosene mixture have been represented in Table S1(supplementary material) and Table 4 . The reported data on each quantity was calculated by taking the average of three measurements for each property. The weights of the chemicals were measured using the CITIZEN digital balance of model CY320C with an accuracy of ±0.0001 g. Prior to the density value measurements, the Ali:HexA DES and kerosene were completely mixed to prepare the binary mixtures. Density was measured by relative mass measurement method. The measurement of dynamic viscosity was conducted by an Anton Paar DSA5000M density and sound velocity meter connected to an Anton Paar Lovis 2000 M micro viscometer, with a precision of 0.5 %. The Dielectric permittivity kit (for liquids) provided by Mittal Enterprises; New Delhi was utilized to determine the dielectric constant of eutectic mixtures as well as diluted DES with kerosene at room temperature. Calibration of instrument was done with air and a reference liquid. The uncertainty of the measurement was determined to be ±0.004. The Abbe Refractometer, model MA-01, supplied by Mittal Enterprises in New Delhi, was used to determine the refractive index of eutectic mixtures at room temperature. To ensure accurate measurements, the instrument was calibrated using double distilled water. The accuracy of the instrument is ±0.0002. The ultrasonic velocity was determined at room temperature and pressure using an ultrasonic interferometer called model F-81. The instrument was operated at a frequency of 2 MHz. The precision of the ultrasonic interferometer was ±0.03 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous and organic phases
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous solution was prepared by dissolving requisite amounts of respective chlorides of Li(0.791 g/L Li), Co(3.413 g/L Co), Ni(0.116 g/L Ni) and 1 M KCl. The selection of these metal concentrations was based on the weight ratio found in the spent Li-ion battery [28]. Metal leaching efficiency is reported as higher in chloride medium when compared to HNO3 and H2SO4 [29]. For the purpose of extraction, the organic phase was prepared by diluting the desired amount of Ali-HexA DES with kerosene. The higher viscosity values of the DESs make them unsuitable for industrial applications. This becomes the major reason for slower mass transfer rate and also slow chemical kinetics, which are not desirable. Mostly in case of solid-liquid dissolution, which occurs during leaching, the process is primarily controlled by diffusion at solid–liquid interface and high viscosity produces obstacles [30]. In this case, therefore diluted form of DES has been used for the extraction purpose. The extraction process was carried out by shaking of equal volumes(10 mL each) of the aqueous and organic phases using a mechanical shaker for a specified duration. Subsequently, both phases were allowed to settle, ensuring complete separation. All extraction experiments were performed at room temperature. The concentrations of Co, and Ni in the feed as well as in raffinate were determined using an ICP-OES (Thermo Fisher Scientific7600 ).

In the process of extraction, the distribution ratio is employed to gain insights into the equilibrium distribution of a solute between two immiscible phases [31]. All extraction experiments were performed in triplicate to avoid experimental error. For the calculation of the distribution ratio, the formula given in Eq. (14) was used.
(14)
D =
[
M
n +
]
organic
[
M
n +
]
raffinate

Where [Mn+]organic is the concentration of metal in the organic phase and [Mn+]raffinate is the concentration of metal in the raffinate after extraction. The percentage of extraction was calculated as given in Eq. (15) and the separation factor described by equation 16.
(15)
% Extraction
= 100 *
[
D
D +
(
Vaqueous
Vorganic
)
]
where Vaqueous is the volume of the aqueous phase and Vorganic is the volume of organic phase. In this experiment Vaqueous =Vorganic.
(16)
The
separation
factor
,
β =
D A
/
D B
Where DA and DB are the distribution ratios of metal A and B, respectively.

Density of binary mixture of Ali-HexA DES increases with increase in mole fraction of DES which is due to the fact that Ali-HexA DES has more density (914 kgm−3) as compared to kerosene (797 kgm−3) as depicted in Table 5 . Viscosity and molar volume values of the mixture show the similar trend with respect to the regular increase in DES mole fraction. DES is more viscous compared to kerosene and addition of more DES causes increase in viscosity of the mixture. This may be due to the structural dissimilarity exist among Ali-HexA DES and kerosene molecules.

The usefulness of dielectric constant data is for the explanation of solvent–solute interaction and it plays a vital role in describing the solvent effects which are very important in the equilibrium chemical dynamics [32]. In addition, the dielectric constant could be used in order to accomplish better control over the chemical reaction when the optimization of reaction environment is done [33]. Close anticipation needs a robust model for the dielectric constant of liquid mixture, relying on temperature, pressure as well as composition of the mixture. In cases of Ali-HexA DES -kerosene binary mixtures dielectric constant values showed a gradual increase with the rise in DES mole fraction. Lichtenecker- Rother mixing model provides better fitting to the experimental dielectric constant values than Karaszewski model as evident from the RMSD values provided in Table 6 . Molar polarization values show regular increase with increase in mole fraction of Ali-HexA DES in the mixture.

For non ideal liquid mixtures, the refractive index of mixing is introduced through thermodynamic approach. A positive change of refractive index upon mixing is understood as follows. The dissimilar London dispersion forces are centred in the unlike molecules constituting the mixture [34]. The refractive index values displayed an orderly increase with the rise in DES mole fraction. The refractive index values for the Ali:HexA DES and kerosene mixture at various mole fractions of DES are consistent using the Gladstone-Dale, Newton, and Arago-Biot models, as indicated in Table 7 . Molar refraction values show regular increase with increase in mole fraction of Ali-HexA DES in the mixture.

The values of ultrasonic velocity of binary mixture increases with increase in DES mole fraction. The different model fittings such as Nomoto, Van Deal, Dannusso, Junjie show that Van Dael model provides the best agreement between the experimental and calculated values as depicted in Table 8 . The interaction parameter, average interaction parameter, chi square value and average percentage error of various ultrasonic model are given in the Table 9 . The adiabatic compressibility (β) and intermolecular free length (Lf) values were gradually decreasing over entire range of mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES as shown in Table 10 . The inclusion of kerosene molecules, interrupted the DES molecular clustering. Both β and Lf values follow similar trend throughout the whole range of composition. This corroborate the existence of less specific interactions among the DES and diluent kerosene molecule when degree of dilution is more. This indicates less compactness of the system in more diluted DES because the components are less closer when there is a decrease in the amount of DES in the binary mixture [35,36]. This is in accordance with the view that the ultrasonic velocities increases with decrease in free length and vice versa [37]. The acoustic impedance regularly increased over the entire range of composition of DES indicating the increase in compactness of the system due to more molecules of DES leading to more solute-solvent interactions [38–40]. Increasing Z values indicated that unlike interactions are less stronger than like interactions. This is in agreement with the theoretical requirements as U and ρ both increase with increase of DES amount in the Ali:HexA-kerosene binary mixture.

Excess molar volume values increase regularly with increase in DES mole fraction showing maximum at 0.329 and then declines as shown in Fig. 3 . These values were negative up to mole fraction 0.04,then became positive. With addition of DES into kerosene,the volume contraction was marked since few molecules of DES were well fitted into interstitial spaces of kerosene molecules. At lower mole fraction DES,the less positive values of excess molar volume indicated that packing efficiency of DES into the kerosene framework was not so efficient and volume expansion is there which shows slight reduction at higher mole fraction of DES since the number of DES molecules increased as compared to those of kerosene. Excess dielectric constant values for the binary mixtures showed a regular increase with mole fraction of DES showing a maxima at 0.329 and were positive over the entire range of mole fraction (Fig. 4 ). This is attributed to the increase in number of effective dipoles which resulted due to parallel alignment of dipoles. The DES and kerosene components in the mixture have weak interaction, as evident from the excess refractive index values, which rises upto mole fraction 0.107 and thereafter decreases as shown in Fig. 5 . The low negative values of excess ultrasonic velocity (UE) in Fig. 6 at all DES mole fraction depicts about weak inter molecular interaction among DES and kerosene molecules. The excess volume, excess dielectric permittivity, excess refractive index and excess acoustic parameters were calculated by fitting into the Redlich Kister polynomial equation and the coefficients obtained therefrom have been given in the Table 11 .

The apparent molar volume of kerosene decreases gradually on addition of DES whereas those of DES increases showing the individual contribution of these components to the non -ideal behavior with reference to the molar volume in the binary mixture as shown in Table 12 .

Plots of Excess intermolecular free length (Lf E), excess isentropic compressibility (βs E) and excess acoustic impedance (ZE) (Figs. 7 , 8 , 9 ) indicate irregular pattern of increasing and decreasing values of the parameters with respect to rise in DES mole fraction. Positive values of Lf E and βs E depict weak interaction between the Ali-HexA DES and diluent kerosene molecules leading to more far approach between the unlike molecules. This is very beneficial from the point of view to use of this binary system for metal extraction purpose. The negative values of ZE are also in accordance with the revelation about presence of weak interactions between the two component molecules.

For mixtures of DES and kerosene with mole fractions of 0.329 and 0.799, the OH stretching frequency was identified at 3410 cm−1. When compared with the Ali:HexA DES, the intensity of the OH stretching frequency lessens as the mole fraction of Ali:HexA DES in mixture reduces, as represented in Fig. 2. In both the Ali:HexA DES and kerosene mixtures, the NCH3 stretching frequency were observed at 1460 cm−1. The intensity of CO stretching frequency at 1172 cm−1 decreases with the decrease in mole fraction of the Ali:HexA DES. These observations show weak interaction without disturbing the DES molecular make up upon dilution.

It has been demonstrated that London dispersion forces operate among dissimilar molecules in ideal mixtures, even though the polarizability of each molecule remains constant [41]. Since these forces are attractive non-specific long-range molecular interactions having origin in induced dipoles, they can be invoked to explain our result that ideal Kirkwood correlation factors vary with composition. In order to speculate about the molecular interaction between DES and kerosene in the binary mixture, the Kirkwood correlation factors were calculated. The values for the binary mixture were found to be more than unity in all cases (Fig. 10 ). This shows the dominance of parallel orientations forming α multimers upon dilution of DES with non-polar kerosene. With rise in DES mole fraction, the g values slowly tend to unity signaling the conversion of few α multimers to β multimers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of Li, Co, and Ni
ce:section.ce:para: In 2015, Van Osch et al., reported hydrophobic DES consisting of hydrogen bond donors, such as carboxylic acid and quaternary ammonium salt, as hydrogen bond acceptor. The hydrophobic nature of DES is attributed to the hydrophobicity of the alkyl chain of HBAs [5,15]. It arises from steric hindrance due to the long alkyl chains which hinders the penetration of water into the core charged group of the quaternary ammonium salt. These hydrophobic DESs, being immiscible in water [15], play a crucial role in biphasic extraction system. In the present investigation, the hydrophobic Ali:Hex A DES was selected for use. As seen from the observed trend of excess molar volume,at a mole fraction of 0.3292, it reached a maxima after which it decreased with further rise in DES mole fraction. This means that volume expansion is highest at X2=0.3292. Therefore, the interaction between DES and diluent kerosene is minimum at this mole fraction which is the ideal condition for extraction purpose. Hence, the mole fraction was varied up to 0.3292 in the solvent extraction experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of shaking time and DES mole fraction
ce:section.ce:para: The duration of shaking indicates the movement of mass from the aqueous phase to the hydrophobic organic phase. Changing the shaking time from 1 to 15 min and varying the mole fraction of DES between 0.02 and 0.3292, resulted in an increase in the percentage of Ni(II) extraction from 18.1 % to 50.1 % at pH 2. Similarly, at pH 5.5, the percentage of Ni(II) extraction increased from 14.9 % to 42.5 %. The data presented in Fig. 11 indicates that the selectivity of Ni(II) extraction is more strongly influenced by pH 2 compared to pH 5.5.

Regarding the extraction of Co(II), rising the shaking time and increasing the mole fraction of DES led to increase in the percentage of extraction from nil to 21.7 % at pH 2 and from nil to 36 % at pH 5.5. Notably, a shaking time of 1 min proved ineffective for Co(II) extraction, as no extraction occurred under this condition. Fig. 12 illustrates that for Co(II) extraction, pH 5.5 yields better extraction results when compared to pH 2.

Concerning the extraction of Li(I), the percentage of extraction experiences a rise from 8.9 % to 24 % when both the shaking time and DES mole fraction were increased at pH 2. In contrast, at pH 5.5, the percentage of Li(I) extraction increased from 0.6 % to 12 %, suggesting a less extraction compared to pH 2, as depicted in Fig. 13 . The equilibrium pH values under these above said conditions were measured as 2.35 for pH 2 and 5.83 for pH 5.5,respectively. The extraction of alkali metals such as lithium needs alkaline medium as reported in literature [42]. This differential activeness towards pH thus shows prospective for efficient separation of Ni from Li.

The separation factor values at different experimental conditions have been given in the Table 13 . Diluted Ali-HexA DES selectively extracted Ni(II) in presence of both Li(I) and Co(II). Complete separation of Ni(II) and Co(II) was achieved under several conditions.

Density,dielectric constant,viscosity,refractive index and ultrasonic velocity values of the binary mixtures of Ali-HexA DES and kerosene have been investigated in this work. The experimentally generated data of dielectric constant,refractive index and ultrasonic velocity were compared with the estimated data collected by using different empirical mixing models. Among all of the empirical rules Van Deal model showed good performance for estimation of ultrasonic velocity of the DES-kerosene mixtures, with an average percentage error of 1.878. Based on obtained results, the Lichtenecker-Rother model showed excellent performance for predicting the dielectric constant of mixtures. The excess properties show non ideality and predict about weak interaction between DES and kerosene at lower mole fraction of DES. The correlation factors higher than one speculate about less compatibility between the DES and kerosene at higher concentration of DES. In case of extraction Ali-HexA DES in kerosene showed better selectivity towards Ni extraction over Li and Co. From the above mentioned results, it is concluded that the dilution of DES with other eco-friendly diluting agents could be more effective for recycling of metal values of spent Li ion batteries. More research is required on the usefulness of these interesting hydrophobic DESs synthesized from non-toxic chemicals for valuable metal recovery from different primary and secondary resources.
Pratyasha Panda: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Methodology. Sujata Mishra: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Siksha ‘O’ Anusandhan Deemed to be University authorities, Bhubaneswar for their kind cooperation to carry out this investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chphi.2023.100447.
